coredata.dc:title: The two dimension carbon quantum dots modified porous g-C3N4/TiO2 nano-heterojunctions for visible light hydrogen production enhancement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Jiaqi
You, Mingzhu
Chi, Chunyan
Dong, Zongjun
Wang, Beibei
Zhu, Mei
Zhao, Weijie
Song, Changsheng
Zheng, Yingying
Li, Chaorong
coredata.dc:description: The dual function carbon quantum dots (C QDs) modified porous g-C3N4/TiO2 two dimension (2D) nano-heterojunctions are prepared by a simple route, through which the porous g-C3N4 nanosheets are prepared by thermal polycondensation, the TiO2 nanoparticles are introduced by hydrothermal method and the C QDs are introduced on the surface of the porous g-C3N4 and TiO2 by coupling successively. The results indicate that TiO2 and C QDs are well combined with the porous g-C3N4. The hydrogen production properties of those 2D nano-heterojunctions are investigated, which exhibit an obvious hydrogen production enhancement (nearly two orders of magnitude) compared with those of the unmodified samples. Through analysis, this enhancement of the photocatalystic hydrogen production is attributed to the dual function C QDs with high catalytic activity of H2O2 decomposition and unique up-conversion photoluminescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic hydrogen production
Overall water splitting
H2O2 decomposition
Up-conversion photoluminescence
2D nano-heterojunctions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the dual function C QDs
Preparation of the dual function C QDs modified g-C3N4/TiO2 2D nano-heterojunctions

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD spectra of the samples at different stage.
The SEM images, (a) porous C3N4 nanosheets, (b) C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions.
The TEM of the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions, (a) the interface of the C3N4 TiO2, (b) the interface of the C3N4 C QDs, (c) HRTEM of the TiO2, (d) HRTEM of the C3N4, (e) HRTEM of the C QDs.
The XPS of the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions, (a) the Ti 2p, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s.
The visible light hydrogen production activity, (a) different samples of C3N4, C3N4 TiO2, C3N4 TiO2 C QDs, C3N4 C QDs and TiO2 C QDs, (b) the histogram of the samples.
The UV–visible absorption spectra of C3N4, C3N4 TiO2, C3N4 TiO2 C QDs, C3N4 -C QDs and TiO2 C QDs.

(a) Up-conversion PL spectra of C QDs with excitation by different wavelengths, (b) schematic illustration for the photocatalystic water splitting hydrogen production enhancement of C3N4 TiO2 C QDs system under visible light.

head.ce:title: The two dimension carbon quantum dots modified porous g-C3N4/TiO2 nano-heterojunctions for visible light hydrogen production enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Taizhou Vocational & Technical College, Taizhou, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dual function carbon quantum dots (C QDs) modified porous g-C3N4/TiO2 two dimension (2D) nano-heterojunctions are prepared by a simple route, through which the porous g-C3N4 nanosheets are prepared by thermal polycondensation, the TiO2 nanoparticles are introduced by hydrothermal method and the C QDs are introduced on the surface of the porous g-C3N4 and TiO2 by coupling successively. The results indicate that TiO2 and C QDs are well combined with the porous g-C3N4. The hydrogen production properties of those 2D nano-heterojunctions are investigated, which exhibit an obvious hydrogen production enhancement (nearly two orders of magnitude) compared with those of the unmodified samples. Through analysis, this enhancement of the photocatalystic hydrogen production is attributed to the dual function C QDs with high catalytic activity of H2O2 decomposition and unique up-conversion photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:para: With the energy demand soaring, the solar energy exploiting, possess enormous, clean and reproducible properties, has been a current research hotspot. Up to now, lots of efficient ways have been carried out, such as, solar lamps, solar building or solar cell, etc [1,2]. Especially the solar hydrogen production, with the pollution free, high chemical energy and storable properties, could compensate the discontinuity of light and has been one of the most efficient ways to exploit the solar energy. Since it was first achieved in 1972 [3], the semiconductor photocatalystic hydrogen production has been reported by lots of researches, such as BiVO4, ZnO, etc [4–6]. Currently, the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [7,8], with non-toxic, physical-chemical stability, low cost and high visible light response (band gap of 2.7 eV), has been the research focus in the field of visible light photocatalystic hydrogen production or degradation [9,10]. However, the efficiency of water splitting hydrogen production by single g-C3N4 is restricted by serious issues of the small specific surface area, high recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, and the H2O2 generates from kinetically competing two-electron reaction would poisons the photocatalysts [11–13].

Based on above issues, lots of works focus on improving the specific surface area, such as the preparation of g-C3N4 nanoparticles, g-C3N4 nanosheets, and so on, and have got series of achievements [14,15]. On the other hand, the heterojuction structure would be an efficient way to decrease the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, and lots of researches have been reported, such as Ge et al. have prepared the MoS2/g-C3N4 heterojuction with prominent hydrogen evolution enhancement [16], Wang et al. have prepared the MnO2-carbon nanotube co-modified C3N4 composite for remarkable visible light hydrogen evolution [17], Wang et al. have obtained the ZnO/g-C3N4 heterojunctions with remarkable photocatalystic performance [18], Hong group have reported a visible-light-driven Ta2O5 modified g-C3N4 heterojunction for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [19], etc [20,21]. It is obvious that the suitable materials are significant for the enhancement. In such semiconductors, the TiO2, possesses stability, preparation easy and environment friendly, has attracted lots of attentions, such as Li et al. have prepared a 2D MoS2/TiO2 heterostructure composite with remarkable visible-light photocatalytic activity [22], Demopoulos et al. have obtained the Cu2ZnSnS4 TiO2 with excellent photocatalysis [23], Viet et al. have obtained SnO2 TiO2 nanotubes with high photocatalytic performance under sunlight [24], Lin et al. have obtained the CuO1-x-TiO2 for enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity [25], and so on [26]. As shown in previous literatures, the band gap of TiO2 are perfectly matched with the g-C3N4, there the conduction band of the TiO2 could accept the electron from the g-C3N4 and the valence band of the TiO2 could transfer the holes to the g-C3N4, which process could increase the carrier life signally for enhancing the photocatalysis [27]. Thus, the porous g-C3N4 with large specific surface area and modified by the TiO2 would be an ideal way to ameliorate the photocatalystic performance.

On the other hand, another serious issue of the reaction intermediate always poisons the photocatalyst, especially the water first oxidized via a two-electron reaction to H2O2, which would poisons the photocatalyst tempestuously and lead to an obvious decreasing in photocatalystic performance [12,28]. Especially in this g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojuction structure, the g-C3N4 is the acceptor of the photongenerated holes, which means that the reaction intermediate of H2O2 would accumulate on its surface, and the strong oxidizability of the H2O2 would poisons the catalyst obviously [29]. Lots of methods, including stirring, bubbling, especially adding chemical agents, have been attempted and got lots inspired results [12,30,31]. However, how to replace the chemical agents would be a great challenge and promising tendency for the photocatalytic water splitting. Fortunately, the carbon quantum dots (C QDs) with dual function would provide researchers a new view to design the photocatalysis for hydrogen production without chemical agents and improve the solar energy utilization further [28,32]. First, as reported by the previous literatures, the C QDs, possess high catalytic activity for H2O2 decomposition [28,33], could promote the H2O2 decompose to H2O and O2 or be used as the electrodes materials, such as Ming groups have prepared the high quality carbon nanodots for enhancing the photocatalytic property [34], Sang et al. have reported the carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes with excellent photoelectrochemical activities [35], that performance could reduce the photocatalystic poisonousness for improving the water splitting hydrogen production. In addition, the C QDs owns unique up-conversion fluorescence would provide researchers another designing for enhancing the photocatalysis [36,37], which could cover the visible light to the ultraviolet or near ultraviolet light for increasing the visible light efficiency. Such as Xu has obtained the carbon-based/ZnO composites for remarkable photocatalysis [38], etc [39]. In this g-C3N4/TiO2 structure, restricted by the band gap (3.2 eV), the TiO2 could hardly utilize the visible light [27]. With the modification of the C QDs, parts of the visible light is covered to ultraviolet light (<380 nm), which could excite the photongenerated carrier of the TiO2 efficiently. So in our design, the chemical catalytic performance is use to decompose the H2O2 for reducing the photocatalystic poisonousness and the up-conversion fluorescence is used to improve the visible light utilization.

In this work, the dual function C QDs modified porous g-C3N4/TiO2 2D nano-heterojunctions with obvious enhancement of visible light water splitting hydrogen production were prepared via a simple route of hydrothermal method and thermal polycondensation. Further, the mechanisms of the enhancement were studied.

All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT, C16H36O4Ti, AR), sucrose (C12H22O11, AR), melamine (C3H6N6, AR) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The deionized water was produced from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system and used throughout the whole experiments.

This processing is multi-step. First, the bulk g-C3N4 was synthesized by thermal polycondensation of melamine. Typically, 10 g of melamine powder was put into an alumina crucible with a cover and heated to 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min and maintained for 4 h by an oven (Hefei Ke Jing Materials Technology Co. Ltd, KSL-1100X).The resultant yellow product was collected and milled into powder. Then, the as-prepared bulk g-C3N4 (1 g) was mixed with 10 mL of H2SO4 (98 wt%) in a 50 mL flask and stirred for 8 h at room temperature. Then the mixture was poured into 100 mL of deionized water slowly and sonicated for exfoliation (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instruments Co. Ltd, KQ-250DB). In this process, the temperature increased rapidly and the color changed from yellow to light yellow. The obtained suspension was then subjected to 10 min of centrifugation at 3000 rpm to remove any un-exfoliated g-C3N4. The obtained light yellow suspension was centrifuged, washed thoroughly with deionized water to remove the residual acid, and finally dried at 80 °C in air for 12 h by an oven (Shanghai Jinghong Experimental Equipment Co. Ltd, DHG-9146A).

In brief, 50 mg as-prepared porous g-C3N4 nanosheets were dispersed into deionized water (20 mL) with sonication for 1 h, and 0.15 mL tetrabutyl titanate was added into the g-C3N4 dispersion to obtain a homogeneous phase after stirring for 60 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 30 mL Teflon-inner-liner stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was kept at 180 °C for 12 h by an oven (Shanghai Jinghong Experimental Equipment Co. Ltd, DHG-9146A). Finally, the precipitate was washed three times respectively with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and dried at 90 °C for 24 h by an oven (Shanghai Jinghong Experimental Equipment Co. Ltd, DHG-9146A). For comparison, pure TiO2 was also prepared by the same procedure without adding g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the dual function C QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 1 g of sucrose was dispersed into 40 mL DI water under magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then the mixture was processed by hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 5 h by an oven (Shanghai Jinghong Experimental Equipment Co. Ltd, DHG-9146A). After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtrated and centrifuged to separate the deposit, and the obtained brown supernate was the highly quality C QDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the dual function C QDs modified g-C3N4/TiO2 2D nano-heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 0.15 g g-C3N4/TiO2 2D nano-heterojunctions were dispersed in 10 mL C-QDs solution (1 mg/mL), and dried at 85 °C for 12 h by an oven (Shanghai Jinghong Experimental Equipment Co. Ltd, DHG-9146A). As the control group, the TiO2 C QDs, C3N4 C QDs were prepared with the similar method.

The phase structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 diffractometer (1.5406 Å, 40 kV and 40 mA). The morphology of the samples was characterized by the Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800). The crystal structure was characterized by the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images (JEM-2100 microscope, 200 kV). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was measured by a Kratos Axis Ultra system with monochromatic Al K X-rays (1486.6 eV) operated at 10 mA and 15 kV. The binding energies were calibrated to the C1 s (284.6 eV) peak. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained by the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi-U3900).

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was performed in Labsolar III system, including the online gas chromatograph (Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co. Ltd.). An outer irradiation-type photoreactor (Pyrex reaction vessel) was connected to a closed gas circulation and an evacuation system. The evolved gases were analyzed by an online gas chromatograph equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and molecular sieve (5 Å pore size). High purity Ar was used as the carrier gas. In the experiment, 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed into 100 mL deionized water with magnetic stirring. Before reaction, the whole system was pumped out to remove the dissolved air. A 300 W Xe lamp, equipped with a UV cut-off filter, was used as the visible light source (>400 nm). A circulation of water with an external cooling coil was conducted to maintain the temperature of suspension at about 25 °C.

Fig. 1 is the XRD spectra of the pure C3N4 nanosheets, C3N4 TiO2, C3N4 TiO2 C QDs, C3N4 C QDs and TiO2 C QDs. As revealed, the only peak present in pure C3N4 nanosheets is corresponded to the (002) plane of the g-C3N4(JCPDS-87-1526) [26]. With the TiO2 being introduced, the new diffraction peaks could be indexed to the (101), (103), (200), (211), (213), (301) and (215) plane of the TiO2(JCPDS-21-1272) [23]. Further, with the C QDs being introduced, the diffraction peaks of the C QDs couldn't be observed, which is attributed to their low crystallinity and concentration [36,39]. The similar results could be observed in the C3N4 C QDs and TiO2 C QDs (As the control groups, the C3N4 C QDs and TiO2 C QDs was also studied, but on account of the pure TiO2 and pure C QDs don't exhibit visible light hydrogen production, they weren't studied solely, including the XRD, absorption and hydrogen production). The analogous diffraction peaks of the C3N4 and TiO2 in different samples indicate that the deposition process would not affect the crystal structure of the samples, which is helpful to explore the photocatalystic mechanism of the nano-heterojunction.

Fig. 2 is the SEM images of the porous C3N4 nanosheets and C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions. As show in Fig. 2a, the as-prepared C3N4 nanosheets are porous and the mesoporous with diameter of about 50–100 nm distribute evenly, which could provide large specific surface area for photocatalytic hydrogen generation and promote the solution convection for increasing the H2O2 diffusing. Fig. 2b is the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions. Restricted by the grain size and distinguishability, the C QDs could not be observed on the surface of the C3N4 and TiO2(so the morphology of the C3N4 C QDs, C3N4 TiO2 and TiO2 C QDs are similar, and the SEM is shown in ESI Fig. S1). As shown, the TiO2 nanoparticles with diameter of about 400–500 nm have attached on the surface of the porous C3N4 nanosheets successfully. Compared with Fig. 2a, the surface of the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions is still porous, which indicates that the deposition of the TiO2 nanoparticles would not destroy the porous structure of the C3N4 nanosheets and could be supported by the XRD.

Fig. 3 is the TEM of the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions. Fig. 3a is the interface of the C3N4 TiO2. As revealed, the diameter of the TiO2 nanoparticle is about 500 nm and corresponds to the SEM. As shown in Fig. 3c, the lattice space of 0.353 nm is corresponded to the (101) plane of TiO2 [40]. Further, the Fig. 3b is the interface of the C3N4 C QDs. As displayed, the C QD has attached on the surface of the C3N4. The HRTEM indicate that the lattice space of 0.329 nm and 0.320 nm is ascribed to the (002) plane of the C3N4 and the (002) plane of the C QD, respectively [26,36].

Furthermore, the components and surface properties of the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions are characterized by XPS and shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a is the high-resolution spectrum of the Ti 2p, as revealed, two obvious peaks at 458.7 eV and 464.5 eV accord with the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of the typical Ti4+, respectively [30,40]. Fig. 4b is the high-resolution spectrum of the C 1s, the main peak could be fitted into three peaks of 284.6eV, 286.0 eV and 287.6 eV, which corresponds to the CC bond, CO bond and CN bond, respectively [30,38,41]. As revealed in Fig. 4c, the N 1s spectrum could be divided into three different nitrogen species at 398.8 eV, 399.4 eV and 400.8 eV, which could be assigned to the CNC, N-(C)3 and CNH, respectively [28,41]. Fig. 4d is the O 1s spectrum, as shown, the peaks locate at 529.7 eV, 531.0 eV and 532.4 eV are ascribed to the TiO, CO and COH bonds [36]. Fig. 4b, 4c and 4d indicate that the TiO2 combine with the C3N4 and C QDs by the CO bond, and the C QDs combine with the C3N4 by CC bond [36,39].
The results XPS, combined with the TEM, indicate that the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions have been prepared successfully.

The visible light photocatalytic hydrogen production by the samples of C3N4, C3N4 TiO2, C3N4 TiO2 C QDs, C3N4 -C QDs and TiO2 C QDs is displayed in Fig. 5 . As revealed, the pure g-C3N4 exhibit a weaker hydrogen production (∼0.068 μmol/g·h), which could be ascribed to the intrinsic hydrogen production property of the g-C3N4. It's obvious that the hydrogen production increases with the TiO2 and C QDs being introduced continuously, and get an optimal value at the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs (∼6.497 μmol/g·h), which increase nearly two orders of magnitude than the unmodified g-C3N4. As the control groups, the C3N4 C QDs and TiO2 C QDs are also tested. As revealed, both of them exhibit obvious enhancement in hydrogen production, and the efficiency is not simple linear accumulation, which indicate that the C3N4/TiO2 heterojuction could play an important role. Compared with previous work, though which is lower than the catalyst modified with the Pt [42,43], but this photocatalyst without sacrificial agent has approached other semiconductor modified photocatalyst with sacrificial agent [44], which may provide a low-cost choice for the design of photocatalyst without sacrificial agent. Further, by comparing the hydrogen production of the C3N4 C QDs and TiO2 C QDs, the C QDs modification is more efficient than the TiO2, which manifest that the decomposition of the H2O2 and catalyst poisonousness may influence the photocatalytic performance more directly. What's more, the oxygen production activity of the samples is shown in Fig. S2. As revealed, the tendency of the oxygen production activity is similar to the hydrogen production activity. By calculating, the ratio of the slope is approximately 2 (In ESI Table 1), which corresponds to the H/O ratio in H2O, and is considered as an evidence for the overall water splitting hydrogen production. All the results are helpful to explain the mechanism of the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs structure. It's obvious that the modification of C QDs and TiO2 could increase the hydrogen production efficiently.

Fig. 6 shows the UV–Visible absorption spectra of samples with different modification. As revealed, the pure C3N4 exhibit obvious visible light absorption locate at approximately 460 nm, which is ascribed to the intrinsic band gap of the C3N4 [26]. After the TiO2 being introduced, the absorption displays an obviously decreasing in visible light region and appears another slope at about 380 nm, which could be ascribed the intrinsic band gap of the TiO2 [29]. Further, with the introducing of the C QDs, the samples (C3N4 TiO2 C QDs and C3N4 -C QDs) exhibit an obvious visible light absorption enhancement, which could be ascribed to the full spectrum absorption of the C QDs [36,41]. This visible light absorption enhancement is benefitted for the hydrogen production.
Therefore, it is significant to explore the mechanism of C QDs and TiO2 modification in this ternary composite structure.

Based on the above results, the mechanism of the photocatalytic water splitting is proposed as the Fig. 7 . As known, restricted by the intrinsic band gap, the TiO2(3.2 eV) couldn't utilize the visible light. Compared with traditional quantum dots, the C QDs with remarkable up-conversion fluorescence could cover the visible light to the ultraviolet or near ultraviolet light via a two-photon process [45,46] are shown in Fig. 7a. As revealed, the up-conversion fluorescence of C QDs with different excitation from 550 to 800 nm exhibit obvious excitation-dependent behavior and mainly locate nearby 360 nm, which could cover the visible light to ultraviolet light effectively. So in this design, the introduced C QDs could be used to cover the visible light to ultraviolet light (<380 nm) for exciting the TiO2. As displayed in Fig. 7b, after the C QDs being introduced, a part of the visible light is converted to ultraviolet light for exciting the photongenerated carrier of the TiO2. Thus, in this C3N4/TiO2 heterojuction, the photongenerated electron-hole pairs from different semiconductor are driven by the heterojuction for separating [47], which process could reduce the recombination of the photongenerated carriers for improving the photocatalytic hydrogen generation, which is considered as a significant reason for the enhancement. Further, as shown in Fig. 7b, the C QDs possess catalytic activity of H2O2 decomposition is deemed as another main reason. As the typical process, the four electron process for water splitting could be described as [28,48]:
(1)
H 2
O =
H 2
+
1 2
O 2
.

While in this C3N4/TiO2 structure, the 2e−/2e− two-step pathway would compete with it, which could be described as [12,28]:
(2)
2
H 2
O =
H 2
O 2
+
H 2
,
(3)
H 2
O 2
=
H 2
O +
1 2
O 2
.

In this process, as the C3N4 is the holes acceptor, that the H2O2 would produce on the surface of them. In this instance, if the H2O2 could not decompose, that would prevent the water splitting further and poison the photocatalyst. With the C QDs are introduced, the H2O2 could be decompose quickly and the water splitting process would proceed continuously, which is considered as the most important reason in this system. In addition, the g-C3N4 possess porous structure is also a considerable reason, because of that porous structure could provide sufficient specific surface area and promote the solution convection for improving the photocatalystic hydrogen production.
With such advantages, the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs exhibit excellent enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production activity than the unmodified samples.

We have successfully prepared the C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions through a simple process and proved its excellent visible light hydrogen production activity. The main reason could be attributed to the dual function C QDs, because that the catalytic activity of H2O2 decomposition could decompose H2O2 for promoting the photocatalytic water splitting processing and reducing the H2O2 poisoning the photocatalyst, the up-conversion fluorescence could cover the visible light to ultraviolet light for improving the light utilization. In addition, heterojuction of C3N4/TiO2 could promote the separation of photongenerated carriers to increase the carrier life, and the porous structure of the g-C3N4 nanosheets could provide plenty of reactive sites are also significant reasons.

The C3N4 TiO2 C QDs 2D nano-heterojunctions with excellent visible light water splitting hydrogen production property are expected as one of the best candidates in solar energy exploiting and environmental protection fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51672249, 51603187 and 91122022), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004 and LY15E030011).

ce:section.ce:para:

mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2018.02.067.
